distractors.trap: Wrong sequence: commercial timing is a real constraint to manage, not ignore.

steps.explanation: The Finance and Governance domains balance cost opportunities with decision quality. Buying now, maximizing discount, or dismissing timing each let procurement override the value decision instead of supporting it.

title: Generative AI Knowledge Base With Hallucination Risk
topic: AI product risk, quality criteria, stakeholder trust, and release governance
scenario: A software company is adding a generative AI assistant to its customer support portal. The prototype reduces search time, but beta users report confident wrong answers for edge cases, and support agents worry customers will stop opening tickets for complex issues. Legal wants disclaimers, product wants a bold launch, and engineering says model tuning will continue after release. The project manager must guide the release around value, quality, and trust.

steps.label: quality bar
steps.prompt: Beta testing reveals confident wrong answers for rare but high-impact cases. What should the project manager do first?

correct.text: Define risk-based acceptance criteria for accuracy, escalation, and the failure modes that matter most
correct.why: The release needs measurable controls for the harms that matter most.

distractors.text: Add a prominent disclaimer and route any uncertain or low-confidence answers to a human agent before allowing the assistant to release
distractors.trap: Premature solution: a disclaimer plus generic routing does not define what accuracy is acceptable.

distractors.text: Tune the model on the failing edge cases and then re-run the beta test
distractors.trap: Symptom not root cause: a technical fix before acceptance criteria define what is good enough.

distractors.text: Commission an independent accuracy audit of the assistant before deciding the release approach
distractors.trap: Wrong sequence: defensible, but it outsources a criteria decision the PM should frame first.

steps.explanation: The Scope, Risk, and Governance domains require fit-for-purpose acceptance criteria tied to impact. A disclaimer, an early retune, or an outsourced audit each act before defining what accuracy is acceptable.

steps.label: agent role
steps.prompt: Support agents fear the assistant will reduce ticket volume but increase escalated customer frustration. What should the project manager do?

correct.text: Engage agents to redesign escalation paths and success metrics that include resolution quality
correct.why: Agents hold the workflow knowledge needed to make the assistant safe and useful.

distractors.text: Reassure agents that staffing decisions sit outside the project's scope and keep the planned launch date on track
distractors.trap: Too narrow: ignoring their workflow concern loses key learning and adoption support.

distractors.text: Give agents authority to override product decisions so they feel ownership
distractors.trap: Premature solution: engagement is not handing governance to one stakeholder group.

distractors.text: Measure success by ticket deflection alone since that is the approved benefit
distractors.trap: Symptom not root cause: deflection without resolution quality creates false benefits.

steps.explanation: The Stakeholders and Resources domains emphasize co-creation and usable operating models. Dismissing the concern, over-delegating governance, or tracking deflection alone each miss customer-resolution value.

steps.label: release
steps.prompt: Launch week arrives with acceptance met for common topics but not for complex billing disputes. What is the best release plan?

correct.text: Release the approved topics with clear human escalation and keep excluded topics in a governed backlog
correct.why: A scoped release delivers value while controlling known high-risk areas.

distractors.text: Launch all topics since user feedback will steadily improve the model
distractors.trap: Premature solution: learning does not justify exposing customers to known high-impact errors.

distractors.text: Cancel the assistant because the excluded billing topic shows it is unsafe
distractors.trap: Too broad: targeted limits can make a partial release acceptable.

distractors.text: Launch and leave the unsupported topics out of the release notes to protect confidence
distractors.trap: Symptom not root cause: hiding the limits undermines trust and appropriate use.

steps.explanation: The Governance and Risk domains support controlled release boundaries. Launching everything, cancelling outright, or hiding the limits each mishandle known high-risk topics that a scoped release contains.

title: Merger Integration With Two Payroll Calendars
topic: merger integration, compliance, change readiness, and employee trust
scenario: After an acquisition, a company must integrate payroll for 9,000 employees across two countries. The acquired company uses different pay cycles, union deductions, and leave rules. The board wants one system within six months to capture synergy savings, but HR warns that payroll errors would destroy employee trust. The project manager inherits a plan built around system cutover dates rather than employee outcomes.

steps.label: readiness lens
steps.prompt: What should the project manager do first after inheriting the cutover-focused plan?

correct.text: Reassess readiness around payroll accuracy, legal pay rules, employee impact, and controls
correct.why: Payroll success depends on trusted outcomes, not merely a system cutover.

distractors.text: Keep the inherited cutover plan but tighten its milestone dates and add buffer so the team still hits the board's six-month synergy savings target
distractors.trap: Symptom not root cause: optimizes speed without confirming payroll readiness.

distractors.text: Ask IT to own readiness since the main deliverable is the system migration
distractors.trap: Too narrow: payroll integration is a business change with legal and employee impacts IT cannot own alone.

distractors.text: Start the first cutover, then measure accuracy and benefits after the initial pay cycle
distractors.trap: Wrong sequence: controls and benefits should shape readiness before cutover, not after.

steps.explanation: The Governance and Stakeholders domains require readiness criteria linked to outcomes. Speeding the cutover, handing readiness to IT, or measuring after go-live each treat payroll as a system event, not a trusted business change.

steps.label: parallel run
steps.prompt: Testing shows a 2 percent pay variance in union deductions during parallel payroll runs. What is the best response?

correct.text: Investigate the variance root cause and hold the affected group out of cutover until criteria are met
correct.why: Known payroll variance must be corrected before exposing employees.

distractors.text: Cut over anyway since 98 percent accuracy is strong for a first parallel run
distractors.trap: Symptom not root cause: small error rates still harm many employees and can breach pay rules.

distractors.text: Process the union deductions manually after launch while the system issue is fixed
distractors.trap: Premature solution: a manual workaround creates control gaps and employee risk.

distractors.text: Ask union leaders to accept temporary variances in exchange for faster integration
distractors.trap: Procedural deflection: known pay errors are not a reasonable trade for speed.

steps.explanation: The Risk and Scope domains support acceptance criteria and root-cause correction. Cutting over, patching manually, or bargaining away the errors each expose employees to a known payroll defect.

steps.label: synergy pressure
steps.prompt: The board asks for the original synergy savings date despite readiness gaps. What should the project manager provide?

correct.text: Present phased and delayed cutover options showing savings, readiness risk, and employee impact
correct.why: Governance needs visible trade-offs to choose the best value path.

distractors.text: Commit to the original synergy date and have the team build detailed contingency and recovery plans to protect it
distractors.trap: Premature solution: committing before readiness evidence misleads decision makers.

distractors.text: Recommend a delay outright because employee trust must outrank the savings target
distractors.trap: Too narrow: the PM should frame trade-offs, not decide on one value dimension.

distractors.text: Present only the savings and dates the board asked about to keep the pack focused
distractors.trap: Symptom not root cause: omitting employee and compliance impact hides material integration risk.

steps.explanation: The Finance, Stakeholders, and Governance domains require balanced decision information. Committing early, deciding by one value, or trimming the pack each deprive governance of the full trade-off.

title: When the Clinical Trial Platform Has Three Masters
topic: hybrid multi-vendor governance and decision rights
scenario: A biotech is building a hybrid clinical-trial operations platform: predictive workstreams for validated infrastructure and audit trails, and agile product teams for investigator-facing workflows. Three vendors own distinct layers (identity, eCRF, analytics), plus an internal platform team. After two release trains, decisions bounce among a steering committee, a validation board, and product owners. Dual-track work is blocked waiting for approvals that no single body owns, and a critical protocol amendment is two weeks from needing system support.

steps.label: Map authority
steps.prompt: The project manager sees decisions stalling across three overlapping forums. What should they do first?

correct.text: Map who decides what, by when, and which forum owns each decision class
correct.why: Ambiguous decision rights are the root cause; clarifying who owns each decision class unblocks hybrid, multi-party delivery.

distractors.text: Have each of the three forums separately document its own remit and current open decisions, then circulate the write-ups so the others can flag where responsibilities overlap or fall through
distractors.trap: Too narrow: each forum still defines its own scope in isolation, so the overlaps and gaps between them go unreconciled even after the write-ups circulate.

distractors.text: Propose merging the three forums into a single decision board to remove the overlap
distractors.trap: Premature solution: jumps to a structural fix before understanding which decisions actually belong where.

distractors.text: Log the stalled decisions as risks and raise them at the next steering committee
distractors.trap: Procedural deflection: defers a live governance gap to the risk log instead of clarifying authority now.

steps.explanation: Governance domain: the blocker is undefined decision rights. Documenting each forum's remit in isolation, restructuring prematurely, or logging it as a risk all dodge the core task of mapping who owns each decision class so work can move.

steps.label: Tailor forums
steps.prompt: The map shows validation needs formal sign-off while workflow UX needs fast product decisions. How should forums be tailored?

correct.text: Use the validation board for validated-system changes and product owners for backlog calls
correct.why: Tailoring matches control intensity to risk: heavy governance for validated systems, fast decisions for product backlog.

distractors.text: Route every regulated and product change through the validation board to keep one standard
distractors.trap: Over-control: applies the strictest control uniformly, killing product agility without improving compliance.

distractors.text: Let product owners approve validated-system changes too, then have the board audit monthly
distractors.trap: Wrong sequence: moves sign-off after the fact for changes that must be validated before release, not after.

distractors.text: Merge the validation board and product owners into one weekly forum to simplify scheduling
distractors.trap: Premature solution: collapsing the forums removes the proportional control the two work types actually need.

steps.explanation: Tailoring and Governance: apply proportional control, strict where regulatory risk is high and fast where product learning dominates. Over-applying, reversing, or merging the forums all break that proportionality.

steps.label: Unblock amendment
steps.prompt: The protocol amendment needs a workflow change next sprint, but the validation board next meets in three weeks. What is the best next action?

correct.text: Classify impact and seek a time-boxed exception through the defined authority
correct.why: Urgency is handled by risk-based impact classification and an agreed exception path, not by skipping quality.

distractors.text: Ask the board chair to approve the change informally so the sprint proceeds
distractors.trap: Too narrow: seeks one person's informal nod outside the defined exception process, bypassing the board's risk assessment.

distractors.text: Convene an emergency full validation session so the board completes its standard multi-reviewer review ahead of the amendment deadline
distractors.trap: Wrong sequence: a full early review is heavier than the risk warrants and may not fit the window a time-boxed exception would.

distractors.text: Log the timing conflict as a risk and wait for the next scheduled board meeting
distractors.trap: Procedural deflection: parks a time-critical clinical need on the calendar instead of using the exception path.

steps.explanation: Value delivery under constraints: use risk-based classification and an agreed exception path within defined authority. An informal chair approval, a full early review, or waiting for the calendar each mishandle a time-critical, quality-sensitive change.

steps.label: Sustain integration
steps.prompt: Vendors still optimize their layers in isolation. What should the project manager institutionalize?

correct.text: A shared definition of done with joint integration checkpoints across all parties
correct.why: Cross-vendor value emerges at integration points; shared quality criteria stop each vendor optimizing only its own layer.

distractors.text: Add an integration test suite the platform team runs after each vendor delivers
distractors.trap: Symptom not root cause: catches defects late in one team's hands instead of building shared ownership of integration up front.

distractors.text: Appoint a dedicated integration manager to chase cross-vendor issues and report a consolidated status to the steering committee each week
distractors.trap: Procedural deflection: a coordinator routes problems to reporting rather than shared quality criteria the vendors own.

distractors.text: Consolidate to a single vendor at renewal to remove the integration seams
distractors.trap: Premature solution: a long commercial move that skips the immediate need for shared integration standards.

steps.explanation: Resources and quality: integration value comes from shared outcomes and joint criteria, not a late test suite, a coordinator who only reports, or a commercial consolidation that dodges the immediate need.

title: Night-Shift Standups and the Quiet Burnout
topic: distributed team equity, working agreements, and sustainable pace
scenario: A fintech product team spans Toronto, Warsaw, and Bengaluru delivering a payments reconciliation service on two-week sprints. Product and engineering leadership sit in Toronto and keep ceremonies at 9:00 a.m. Eastern so executives can attend. Warsaw and Bengaluru members join late evening or early morning for weeks. Velocity is flat, defect escapes rose, and a Bengaluru engineer privately says people are exhausted but fear looking uncommitted if they push back. The project manager is asked to fix delivery quality without moving the product roadmap.

steps.label: Surface the issue
steps.prompt: What should the project manager do first?

correct.text: Create a safe forum on meeting-time impacts, then co-create options with the team
correct.why: Servant leadership: surface the real fatigue and constraints safely before redesigning how the team collaborates.

distractors.text: Rotate ceremony times across the three regions before hearing how the impact is distributed
distractors.trap: Premature solution: a fair-sounding fix applied before understanding where the burden actually falls.

distractors.text: Send an anonymous engagement survey across the whole company and wait to act until the results are collected and statistically analyzed
distractors.trap: Wrong sequence: a slow, broad instrument delays engaging the team that has already raised the issue directly.

distractors.text: Ask the Bengaluru engineer who spoke up to propose a new schedule for everyone
distractors.trap: Too narrow: loads one person with the fix and skips co-designing with the whole distributed team.

steps.explanation: People domain and servant leadership: create safety and co-design with the whole team. Jumping to a rotation, launching a slow survey, or delegating the fix to one engineer all skip shared diagnosis.

steps.label: Redesign ceremonies
steps.prompt: The team agrees fatigue is real. Which approach best balances inclusion and delivery?

correct.text: Rotate sync windows, use async status, and reserve rare all-hands for true decisions
correct.why: Tailored collaboration cuts chronic off-hours load while keeping live sync for decisions that truly need it.

distractors.text: Record every Toronto meeting and post the videos with notes for off-hours members to watch overnight and reply with comments the next day
distractors.trap: Symptom not root cause: an async dump still centers one region and delays feedback rather than reducing off-hours load.

distractors.text: Split into three regional teams with separate backlogs to avoid cross-timezone work
distractors.trap: Premature solution: fragmenting the backlog creates integration debt and loses shared ownership.

distractors.text: Rotate who facilitates each region's session while keeping the current daily sync cadence
distractors.trap: Too narrow: changing the host leaves the underlying off-hours meeting load fully in place.

steps.explanation: Resources and Stakeholders: sustainable distributed work blends async with rotated sync. Recordings, separate regional backlogs, or a rotating host each leave the off-hours burden or shared ownership problem unsolved.

steps.label: Protect quality
steps.prompt: Defect escapes correlate with handoffs at 1 a.m. local time. What should change in the delivery system?

correct.text: Align reviews and testing to overlapping, well-rested hours and adjust WIP limits
correct.why: Quality is a system property; moving critical review and testing into well-rested overlap hours reduces escapes.

distractors.text: Add an automated regression gate that blocks merges failing the overnight test run
distractors.trap: Symptom not root cause: more automated gating catches some defects but leaves the fatigued-handoff cause in place.

distractors.text: Add a dedicated Toronto QA pass that retests everything delivered overnight and signs it off before each release
distractors.trap: Inspection over prevention: a downstream retest layer adds cost and rework without fixing the handoff design.

distractors.text: Shorten sprints to one week so defects surface faster within each iteration
distractors.trap: Premature solution: compresses cadence without addressing when careful work happens, often raising ceremony load.

steps.explanation: Schedule and quality under value delivery: design the flow so critical gates happen when people are rested. Extra automated gates, a downstream QA retest, or shorter sprints treat symptoms rather than the handoff timing.

steps.label: Hold the line
steps.prompt: A VP insists the old 9 a.m. Eastern all-hands return because it is convenient for the steering committee. How should the project manager respond?

correct.text: Share quality and wellbeing evidence, offer a short steering demo, keep team agreements
correct.why: Stakeholder engagement: use evidence and a tailored forum to meet the VP's need without reversing sustainable agreements.

distractors.text: Escalate the disagreement to the steering committee with the data and hold the new team agreements in place only until the committee rules
distractors.trap: Procedural deflection: kicks a live, evidenced decision upstairs and suspends a working solution while waiting.

distractors.text: Offer to restore the 9 a.m. all-hands twice a month as a middle ground
distractors.trap: Premature solution: concedes a partial return to the harmful slot before presenting evidence that another forum works.

distractors.text: Meet the VP's need by having only Toronto leads attend live and briefing the rest
distractors.trap: Too narrow: solves the VP's convenience by re-centering one region and cutting others out of the decision.

steps.explanation: Stakeholder engagement and ethics: meet governance needs with data and a tailored forum, not by escalating and freezing a working agreement, conceding a partial return before the evidence, or re-centering one region.

title: The Scope Was Green Until the Assurance Audit
topic: ESG claims, evidence integrity, and scope for external assurance
scenario: A consumer-goods company is mid-build on a carbon-accounting platform intended to support public net-zero claims and future limited assurance. Engineering velocity looks healthy and the product owner prioritizes dashboards that impress the board. An internal sustainability auditor samples data lineage and finds key emissions factors are hard-coded, supplier data is incomplete, and change history for calculation rules is not retained. Marketing has already drafted campaign language that depends on the platform numbers. Leadership wants the launch date held.

steps.label: Reframe success
steps.prompt: What should the project manager emphasize first with the sponsor and product owner?

correct.text: Value is defensible emissions insight and audit-ready lineage, not dashboard polish
correct.why: For ESG claims, value is defensible insight and audit-ready evidence, not cosmetic dashboard outputs.

distractors.text: Frame success as passing the internal auditor's next data-lineage sample on time
distractors.trap: Too narrow: fixates on one audit checkpoint rather than the broader outcome of defensible, usable emissions insight.

distractors.text: Commit to a focused remediation sprint on the hard-coded emissions factors first, then align the sponsor and product owner on what success means
distractors.trap: Premature solution: jumps to fixing hard-coded factors before the sponsor and product owner agree the value definition.

distractors.text: Escalate the audit findings to the risk committee and let them define success criteria
distractors.trap: Procedural deflection: routes the value conversation to a committee instead of aligning the accountable sponsor and owner now.

steps.explanation: Business Environment and value: with external claims looming, redefine success around defensible outcomes and audit-ready evidence. Fixating on one audit sample, remediating before aligning, or deferring the definition to a committee all miss that reframing.

steps.label: Reprioritize backlog
steps.prompt: How should the near-term backlog be reordered?

correct.text: Elevate lineage, rule versioning, and supplier data quality before new visuals
correct.why: Risk-based prioritization puts the enabling controls for assurance ahead of nonessential presentation work.

distractors.text: Finish the board dashboard this sprint, then tackle lineage and versioning next
distractors.trap: Wrong sequence: ships impressive but untrustworthy numbers first, exactly the ordering the audit warns against.

distractors.text: Pause features and stand up an enterprise data-warehouse program to fix data once
distractors.trap: Over-scoping: a large multi-year build ignores incremental value and the near-term assurance risk.

distractors.text: Prioritize only supplier data completeness this quarter since that is the auditor's single largest finding by record count
distractors.trap: Too narrow: fixes one finding while leaving rule versioning and lineage, also required for assurance, unaddressed.

steps.explanation: Scope and Risk: sequence the enabling controls, lineage, versioning, and supplier quality, ahead of polish. Dashboard-first, an over-scoped warehouse program, or fixing only one finding each mishandle the assurance risk.

steps.label: Guard the claim
steps.prompt: Marketing wants to publish campaign numbers next month regardless. What is the project manager's best action?

correct.text: Share a readiness assessment with marketing and legal; limit claims to supported evidence
correct.why: Ethics and stakeholder engagement: tie external claims to actual capability by aligning marketing and legal on readiness.

distractors.text: Send marketing a written note on the specific data gaps and let them decide what to publish
distractors.trap: Too narrow: informing one party without legal or a shared readiness view leaves the claim decision ungoverned.

distractors.text: Raise the claims risk at the next steering committee before contacting marketing
distractors.trap: Procedural deflection: defers a near-term publication decision to a committee cycle instead of engaging the parties who can constrain the claim.

distractors.text: Draft approved claim language yourself and hand marketing only that copy
distractors.trap: Premature solution: writes the fix before legal and marketing assess what the evidence actually supports.

steps.explanation: Ethics and Business Environment: align external claims to supported evidence by engaging marketing and legal on readiness. Informing marketing alone, deferring to a committee, or pre-writing the copy each fall short of governed, truthful communication.

steps.label: Stage the release
steps.prompt: Leadership still wants a launch date. Which release strategy best protects value?

correct.text: Release internal decision-support with limits first; gate public metrics until controls pass
correct.why: Staged release delivers internal value now while withholding public metrics until the controls are proven.

distractors.text: Launch publicly with a prominent methodology caveat describing the known data limits
distractors.trap: Symptom not root cause: a visible caveat addresses appearances but does not make the numbers defensible for marketed claims.

distractors.text: Run a limited external beta with select customers before the controls pass
distractors.trap: Wrong sequence: exposes real external users to weak numbers ahead of the controls that make them defensible.

distractors.text: Release the full platform internally and externally at the same time, then harden the lineage and versioning controls in place afterward
distractors.trap: Premature solution: treats internal and public exposure as the same risk, releasing public claims before controls exist.

steps.explanation: Value delivery and risk: stage so usable internal outcomes ship while higher-risk public claims wait for controls. A prominent caveat, an early external beta, or a simultaneous full release all expose public claims prematurely.

title: The Robotics Integrator That Stopped Integrating
topic: procurement performance, contract remedies, and collaborative recovery
scenario: A logistics firm hired a systems integrator under a firm-fixed-price contract to deliver warehouse robotics orchestration with milestone payments. By month five, site acceptance tests fail repeatedly: interfaces with the warehouse management system are incomplete, and the integrator blames late customer data maps while the customer blames the integrator's thin architecture staff. The contract has liquidated damages, but invoking them would likely freeze cooperation just as peak season approaches. The project manager must recommend a path that protects schedule and value.

steps.label: Diagnose jointly
steps.prompt: What is the best first move?

correct.text: Run joint fact-finding on obligations, acceptance criteria, and actual blockers
correct.why: Shared factual diagnosis separates real dependencies from blame before choosing contractual or technical remedies.

distractors.text: Issue a formal cure notice citing the failed acceptance tests and set a deadline
distractors.trap: Premature remedy: invokes a contractual lever before a shared diagnosis of the mutual causes.

distractors.text: Have your architecture team audit the integrator's interface design and report gaps
distractors.trap: Too narrow: a one-sided review examines only the vendor's contribution, missing the late data maps on your side.

distractors.text: Extend the timeline and add senior interface engineers to the integrator's team before confirming what is actually blocking the acceptance tests
distractors.trap: Premature solution: buys schedule and headcount before knowing the root cause, which may not be capacity.

steps.explanation: Procurement and Risk: build a shared baseline of facts and obligations first. A cure notice, a one-sided technical audit, or adding time and staff all act before the mutual root causes are understood.

steps.label: Choose remedies
steps.prompt: Fact-finding shows both sides missed obligations: customer data maps late by three weeks; integrator understaffed interface design. What next?

correct.text: Negotiate a recovery plan with staffing, deliverables, and measurable interface gates
correct.why: Collaborative recovery with measurable commitments realigns both parties while preserving a path to value.

distractors.text: Apply partial liquidated damages for the integrator's share of the delay and otherwise leave the current delivery plan unchanged
distractors.trap: Symptom not root cause: penalizes one side but creates no added capacity or fix for the mutual failure.

distractors.text: Restructure the contract to time-and-materials with a not-to-exceed cap and proceed
distractors.trap: Premature solution: a commercial re-paper before agreeing the concrete recovery actions and gates.

distractors.text: Reset the acceptance criteria downward so current progress passes, then continue
distractors.trap: Wrong sequence: loosening criteria to clear the gate skips fixing why the interfaces fail.

steps.explanation: Procurement tailoring: when both sides missed obligations, negotiate a measurable recovery. Partial damages alone, a premature contract re-paper, or loosened acceptance criteria each dodge the mutual capacity and interface fixes.

steps.label: Protect peak season
steps.prompt: Peak season is nine weeks out and full automation will miss it. How should scope and schedule be adapted?

correct.text: Define a minimum viable automation slice for peak-critical zones, then a follow-on tranche
correct.why: Slicing to a peak-critical increment captures partial benefit when the full baseline is no longer realistic.

distractors.text: Add contractor crews and overtime shifts to hold the full automation scope for peak
distractors.trap: Premature crash: buys effort to save the whole scope without a controlled, safe plan or proof it fits the window.

distractors.text: Automate the single highest-volume zone fully and leave the others fully manual
distractors.trap: Too narrow: optimizes one zone rather than the set of peak-critical zones the season actually needs.

distractors.text: Hold the full baseline and rebaseline only after peak season has passed
distractors.trap: Wrong sequence: preserving the plan on paper guarantees zero seasonal value when it inevitably slips.

steps.explanation: Scope and Schedule under value delivery: replan to a viable peak-critical increment. Crashing to hold full scope, automating only one zone, or keeping the doomed baseline each forfeit safe, realistic seasonal value.

steps.label: Govern going forward
steps.prompt: What governance change best reduces recurrence?

correct.text: Add joint interface reviews and early integration demos as payment and acceptance gates
correct.why: Tying early integration proofs to acceptance and payment surfaces interface risk long before site testing.

distractors.text: Increase status reporting frequency and add a weekly cross-vendor integration risk review
distractors.trap: Symptom not root cause: more reporting visibility does not verify that interfaces actually work early.

distractors.text: Add a penalty clause for each future missed acceptance test to the contract
distractors.trap: Too narrow: stronger penalties punish late failure but do not surface integration risk earlier.

distractors.text: Require a full integration test only at the final milestone before go-live
distractors.trap: Wrong sequence: concentrating verification at the end repeats the late-discovery failure.

steps.explanation: Governance and Risk: bake integration proofs into acceptance and payment events. More status meetings, harsher penalty clauses, or a single end-stage integration test all leave interface risk to surface late.

title: The Hiring Model That Learned the Wrong Lesson
topic: AI ethics, bias risk, and responsible release decisions
scenario: A global employer is piloting an AI-assisted screening tool on a hybrid project: model training and compliance under predictive gates, UX under agile increments. Early metrics show faster screening and recruiter satisfaction. A fairness review then finds disparate impact against candidates from certain regions and non-native English names, partly driven by historical hiring labels in the training set. Legal has not banned the pilot, but employee relations warns of trust damage. The sponsor wants to expand to five more countries next quarter.

steps.label: Treat the signal
steps.prompt: How should the project manager treat the fairness findings?

correct.text: Treat them as material risks, pause expansion, and bring accountable owners into the response
correct.why: Material bias threatens value, reputation, and ethics; it belongs in governed risk handling before any scale-up.

distractors.text: Direct the data team to rebalance the training set before assessing the disparity's impact
distractors.trap: Premature solution: jumps to a technical fix before framing impact or engaging accountable owners.

distractors.text: Ask legal whether the disparate impact creates liability and proceed on their answer
distractors.trap: Too narrow: treats a fairness and value issue as a legal-only question, skipping ethics and product accountability.

distractors.text: Commission an external bias audit and continue running the current pilot unchanged while it proceeds
distractors.trap: Wrong sequence: outsources and delays a decision the PM should frame now while exposure continues.

steps.explanation: Risk and Business Environment: treat AI fairness findings as material risks needing a governed, multi-party response before scaling. Rebalancing data first, asking only legal, or auditing while the pilot runs each act before framing the impact.

steps.label: Choose mitigation
steps.prompt: Which mitigation package is most aligned with responsible delivery?

correct.text: Improve data practices, keep humans in the loop, and gate release on fairness thresholds
correct.why: Responsible AI combines data fixes, human accountability, and explicit fairness gates rather than any single lever.

distractors.text: Retrain the model on rebalanced data and redeploy it once overall accuracy recovers
distractors.trap: Too narrow: a purely technical fix without human oversight or fairness gates can leave subgroup harm unmeasured.

distractors.text: Add a fairness threshold gate now and defer the human-in-the-loop and data work
distractors.trap: Premature partial: picks one control and ships before the complementary safeguards exist.

distractors.text: Give recruiters a confidence caveat on each score and keep the model unchanged
distractors.trap: Symptom not root cause: disclosure informs users but does not remediate disparate impact.

steps.explanation: Quality and ethics in AI: mitigation needs data improvement, human accountability, and measurable fairness gates together. Retraining alone, a single gate, or a confidence caveat each cover only part of responsible delivery.

steps.label: Reset expansion
steps.prompt: The sponsor still pushes country expansion next quarter. What recommendation is best?

correct.text: Expand only after fairness gates pass and each jurisdiction completes legal review
correct.why: Geographic scale is a controlled release: expand only where fairness gates pass and local legal review clears.

distractors.text: Expand on the sponsor's timeline in parallel with the fairness remediation work
distractors.trap: Premature solution: scales before the gates confirm the measured harm is controlled.

distractors.text: Expand first to jurisdictions that share the pilot's existing legal regime, deferring fairness-gate sign-off until after those launches
distractors.trap: Wrong sequence: reuses one legal read to move fast while skipping the fairness gate that should precede scale.

distractors.text: Present the expansion decision to the ethics board and expand once they respond
distractors.trap: Procedural deflection: defers a decision the PM should frame with clear gate criteria to a committee's pace.

steps.explanation: Governance of releases: stage expansion behind fairness and legal-readiness gates per jurisdiction. Scaling in parallel, reusing one legal regime, or waiting on a committee each move before the evidence supports it.

steps.label: Engage workers
steps.prompt: Recruiters fear the tool will be used to judge their headcount decisions. What should the project manager do?

correct.text: Co-design role boundaries and transparency so the tool supports judgment, not silent override
correct.why: Adoption depends on co-designed decision rights and transparency so the tool augments rather than overrides recruiters.

distractors.text: Publish a policy that fixes exactly when recruiters may override the model's recommendation before consulting the recruiters themselves
distractors.trap: Premature solution: sets the rules unilaterally instead of co-designing boundaries with the people affected.

distractors.text: Run a training session explaining the model so recruiters trust the scores more
distractors.trap: Too narrow: communication alone addresses trust without settling the real decision rights.

distractors.text: Escalate recruiter concerns to HR leadership and await a formal usage directive
distractors.trap: Procedural deflection: routes a workable co-design task to a directive from above.

steps.explanation: People and Stakeholders: adoption needs co-designed decision rights and transparency. A unilateral override policy, a training session, or escalating for a directive each skip engaging recruiters in the design.

title: Outputs Delivered, Benefits Orphaned
topic: benefits ownership, transition, and outcome accountability
scenario: A government agency project closes a citizen-services portal on time. The charter promised reduced call-center volume and higher self-service completion. At closure, the system works, training materials exist, and the project team is released. Three months later call volume is unchanged. Investigation shows business units never staffed content upkeep, call-center scripts still push phone resolution, and no one accepted the benefits owner role after the original sponsor retired. A new executive asks the former project manager, now reassigned, to explain why benefits failed.

steps.label: Separate output vs outcome
steps.prompt: How should the former project manager frame what happened?

correct.text: Outputs were delivered, but outcomes failed because benefits ownership and adoption lapsed
correct.why: Separating outputs from outcomes locates the failure in benefits realization and lapsed change ownership.

distractors.text: Adoption failed because the staff training materials were not engaging or detailed enough
distractors.trap: Symptom not root cause: blames one artifact rather than the missing benefits ownership and incentives.

distractors.text: The handoff failed because operations were not given a detailed enough runbook
distractors.trap: Too narrow: reduces a benefits-governance gap to a documentation shortfall.

distractors.text: Benefits stalled only because measurement started too late to show the improvement
distractors.trap: Wrong diagnosis: treats a metrics-timing issue as the cause, missing unstaffed upkeep and misaligned scripts.

steps.explanation: Business Environment: benefits are outcomes realized in operations, not automatic results of accepted deliverables. Blaming training quality, a thin runbook, or late measurement each mistake a symptom for the ownership and adoption gap.

steps.label: Repair ownership
steps.prompt: What should be recommended to recover benefits now?

correct.text: Name a benefits owner, fund the operational change, and track outcomes with ops
correct.why: Benefits recovery needs an accountable owner, funded operational change, and measured outcomes after delivery.

distractors.text: Assign content upkeep to an existing analyst and revisit the metrics quarterly
distractors.trap: Too narrow: staffs one gap without an accountable benefits owner or aligned operations.

distractors.text: Rewrite the call-center scripts to actively push self-service resolution before naming anyone to own the benefit
distractors.trap: Premature solution: fixes one visible driver before establishing ownership and funded change.

distractors.text: Escalate the benefits shortfall to the new executive and await a funding decision
distractors.trap: Procedural deflection: reports the gap upward instead of proposing the ownership-and-change package.

steps.explanation: Value delivery continues after handoff through accountable ownership, funded operational change, and outcome measurement. Staffing one task, rewriting scripts first, or escalating for funds each address only a piece.

steps.label: Fix transition design
steps.prompt: For future programs, what transition practice should be embedded before closure?

correct.text: A benefits plan with named owners, KPIs, and exit criteria for sustaining readiness
correct.why: Closure should verify transition readiness for outcomes, not only technical acceptance of deliverables.

distractors.text: A mandatory operations sign-off confirming frontline staff are fully trained and ready before the delivery team disbands
distractors.trap: Too narrow: confirms people are ready but not owned benefits, KPIs, or sustaining funding.

distractors.text: A post-implementation review scheduled six months after every project closes
distractors.trap: Wrong sequence: a later review inspects outcomes after the fact rather than designing ownership before closure.

distractors.text: A richer handover pack documenting every process the operations team must run
distractors.trap: Symptom not root cause: more documentation does not create accountable benefits ownership.

steps.explanation: Governance of transition: define benefits ownership, KPIs, and sustaining-readiness exit criteria before closure. A training sign-off, a later post-implementation review, or a fuller handover pack each stop short of designed ownership.

steps.label: Align incentives
steps.prompt: Call-center leaders are measured only on average handle time, which discourages promoting self-service. What should happen?

correct.text: Work with leadership to realign operational KPIs with the intended self-service outcomes
correct.why: Misaligned incentives sabotage benefits; operational measures must reinforce the intended outcomes.

distractors.text: Coach agents to mention self-service on every call while the handle-time target stays
distractors.trap: Symptom not root cause: behavior nudges cannot overcome the measure that penalizes them.

distractors.text: Add a self-service referral metric on top of the existing handle-time target
distractors.trap: Too narrow: stacking a second metric leaves the conflicting incentive in place.

distractors.text: Report the incentive conflict to the operations director with the data and let them decide how to adjust the measures
distractors.trap: Procedural deflection: flags the misalignment upward rather than working to realign the measures.

steps.explanation: Organizational alignment: realign the operational measures with the value thesis. Coaching agents, adding a second metric over the old target, or reporting the conflict upward each leave the punishing incentive intact.

title: The Portfolio That Ate the Critical Path
topic: resource contention, negotiation without authority, and prioritization
scenario: In a matrix organization, four projects share one identity-and-access engineering pool. Your regulated customer onboarding project is on the critical path for a contractual go-live. The pool manager keeps pulling the same senior engineer onto a flashy internal AI demo for an executive showcase. Your risk register already flags this dependency as high. Sprint commitments slip twice. The functional manager says every project is priority one. Your sponsor wants results, not excuses.

steps.label: Make conflict visible
steps.prompt: What should the project manager do first?

correct.text: Quantify schedule and contract impact, then bring options to sponsor and portfolio forums
correct.why: Without formal authority, influence rests on transparent impact data taken to the right prioritization forums.

distractors.text: Negotiate directly with the pool manager to lock the senior engineer to your project
distractors.trap: Too narrow: a side agreement with one manager skips the portfolio forum that must weigh all four projects.

distractors.text: Propose a specific reallocation of the senior engineer to your project directly to the sponsor before quantifying the schedule and contract impact
distractors.trap: Premature solution: asks for a fix before the schedule and contract data make the case.

distractors.text: Raise the recurring conflict as a new risk and wait for the risk review to address it
distractors.trap: Procedural deflection: logs a live contention issue instead of escalating it with quantified impact now.

steps.explanation: Resources and Stakeholders: surface contention with quantified impact through the right forum. A side deal with the pool manager, a premature reallocation ask, or logging it as a risk each bypass proper prioritization.

steps.label: Seek a decision
steps.prompt: The portfolio board hears the case. What outcome should the project manager advocate?

correct.text: An explicit priority ranking and capacity allocation for the shared pool
correct.why: Shared-capacity conflicts need explicit prioritization and allocation, not declarations that every project is priority one.

distractors.text: A guarantee that your regulated project gets the engineer full-time until go-live
distractors.trap: Too narrow: fixes your project's need without a portfolio ranking, recreating contention for the others.

distractors.text: A decision to hire two contractors so the pool stops being a constraint
distractors.trap: Premature solution: adds capacity before the board sets priorities among competing demands.

distractors.text: A standing weekly conflict-resolution meeting to adjudicate resource clashes among the four projects as they arise
distractors.trap: Procedural deflection: institutionalizes recurring firefighting instead of an explicit priority decision.

steps.explanation: Governance and Resources: shared capacity needs explicit prioritization tied to strategy and commitments. A full-time guarantee for one project, hiring contractors first, or a standing clash meeting each avoid the priority call.

steps.label: Tailor plan B
steps.prompt: The board ranks your project higher but the senior engineer is still 50 percent available for two weeks. What is best?

correct.text: Replan to available capacity, protect critical-path tasks, and pair to grow backup skill
correct.why: Replanning to real capacity while pairing to build a backup honors constraints and cuts single-point risk.

distractors.text: Assign the senior engineer only to the critical-path tasks and defer everything else on the plan
distractors.trap: Too narrow: protects the path but builds no backup, leaving the key-person risk intact.

distractors.text: Bring a junior engineer up to speed first, then replan once they are productive
distractors.trap: Wrong sequence: front-loads ramp-up time the two-week window cannot absorb before protecting the path.

distractors.text: Keep the current plan but add buffer to each sprint to absorb the shortfall
distractors.trap: Symptom not root cause: padding estimates hides the capacity gap without reprioritizing the critical path.

steps.explanation: Schedule and Resources: replan to actual capacity and reduce key-person risk through pairing. Loading only the expert, ramping a junior first, or padding buffers each fail to both protect the path and build backup.

steps.label: Prevent recurrence
steps.prompt: What systemic fix should be proposed after this fire drill?

correct.text: A visible capacity model for critical skills with intake limits when saturated
correct.why: Chronic thrashing is a system-design problem; intake must match the capacity of constrained critical skills.

distractors.text: A policy reserving the senior identity engineer for regulated projects only
distractors.trap: Too narrow: protects one person for one project type without managing overall critical-skill capacity.

distractors.text: A hiring plan to add three identity engineers so the pool is never constrained
distractors.trap: Premature solution: assumes more headcount before modeling where the real capacity limits sit.

distractors.text: A monthly portfolio review to spot and unwind resource conflicts after they have already occurred
distractors.trap: Procedural deflection: reviews contention retrospectively rather than limiting intake to real capacity.

steps.explanation: Portfolio resource governance: match intake to the real capacity of scarce critical skills. Reserving one engineer, hiring blindly, or a retrospective monthly review each miss the capacity-based intake control.

title: Handoffs That Turned Green Builds Red
topic: quality system design across onshore-offshore flow
scenario: A hybrid insurance platform uses onshore product ownership and architecture with an offshore delivery center for build and most testing. Escape defects at UAT have doubled. Analysis shows stories accepted in sprint reviews still fail business scenarios because examples are ambiguous, environments drift, and overnight handoffs lose context. Leadership proposes adding an onshore inspection team to retest everything. The project manager suspects the system of work, not individual effort, is the problem.

steps.label: See the system
steps.prompt: What should the project manager do before adding inspectors?

correct.text: Map end-to-end flow and defect discovery to find where ambiguity and handoff loss enter
correct.why: Quality problems are usually systemic; process mapping finds leverage points before adding inspection cost.

distractors.text: Move build and most testing onshore in phases to shorten the overnight handoff loop
distractors.trap: Premature solution: re-locates work to treat a symptom before confirming where defects actually enter.

distractors.text: Have architects review each escaped defect and brief the offshore leads on the gaps
distractors.trap: Too narrow: a one-sided design review looks at outputs without mapping the flow that produced them.

distractors.text: Add a fourth environment so more parallel testing catches defects before UAT
distractors.trap: Symptom not root cause: more environments alone do not fix ambiguous acceptance criteria.

steps.explanation: Quality as system design: diagnose flow and handoffs before investing in more inspection layers. Relocating work, one-sided design reviews, or extra environments all act before the systemic causes are mapped.

steps.label: Shift left
steps.prompt: The map shows ambiguous examples and late clarification. Which change best improves first-time quality?

correct.text: Use shared example-based acceptance criteria and overlapping clarification before build
correct.why: Prevention and early shared understanding beat late inspection for distributed delivery.

distractors.text: Require a detailed specification document for every story before any coding begins
distractors.trap: Symptom not root cause: heavier docs can still be ambiguous and slow feedback without shared examples.

distractors.text: Extend the offshore day so a few live hours overlap onshore for clarifying questions
distractors.trap: Too narrow: more overlap helps timing but does not remove the ambiguity in the examples themselves.

distractors.text: Add a business-analyst review gate that signs off examples after the sprint review
distractors.trap: Wrong sequence: reviewing examples after build clarifies too late to prevent the escapes.

steps.explanation: Scope and Quality: shared acceptance examples clarified before build prevent defects. Heavier specs, more overlap hours alone, or a late sign-off gate each fall short of early shared understanding.

steps.label: Stabilize environments
steps.prompt: Environment drift causes intermittent failures. What is the most appropriate action?

correct.text: Treat environment configuration as controlled product work with ownership and promotion rules
correct.why: Uncontrolled environments are a quality risk; configuration needs explicit ownership and control.

distractors.text: Have a dedicated engineer manually refresh and reconcile every test environment at the start of each day before work begins
distractors.trap: Symptom not root cause: manual daily fixes chase drift without controlling how configuration changes.

distractors.text: Flag intermittently failing tests so the board sees a stable trend while drift is investigated
distractors.trap: Procedural deflection: managing the status signal defers the live instability instead of controlling it.

distractors.text: Add retries to intermittent tests so transient environment failures stop blocking the pipeline
distractors.trap: Symptom masking: retries hide instability rather than fixing the uncontrolled configuration.

steps.explanation: Risk and quality governance: environments are part of the delivery system and need configuration control. Manual morning fixes, reclassifying flaky tests, or auto-retries each mask drift instead of controlling it.

steps.label: Respond to leadership
steps.prompt: Leadership still wants a large onshore inspection army. How should the project manager advise?

correct.text: Pilot prevention practices with metrics; use inspection only as a temporary safety net
correct.why: Cost-of-quality theory favors investing primarily in prevention, with inspection as a bridge, not permanent appraisal bloat.

distractors.text: Stand up the onshore inspection team now and revisit prevention once escapes fall
distractors.trap: Wrong sequence: builds a permanent appraisal layer first and defers the prevention that fixes the cause.

distractors.text: Stand up a smaller onshore inspection cell focused only on the highest-severity UAT escapes for now
distractors.trap: Too narrow: a targeted inspection cell still treats symptoms and leaves the systemic causes unaddressed.

distractors.text: Escalate the disagreement to the steering committee and pause changes until it decides
distractors.trap: Procedural deflection: routes a decision the PM can frame with evidence to a committee while escapes continue.

steps.explanation: Cost of quality and change leadership: prefer prevention with measured improvement, using inspection as a bridge. Standing up appraisal first, a smaller inspection cell, or escalating for a ruling each favor inspection over prevention.

title: Shadow Steering After the Sponsor Vanished
topic: sponsor engagement failure and illegitimate decision channels
scenario: Midway through a hospital revenue-cycle modernization, the executive sponsor takes an unexpected leave. No deputy is named. A charismatic department head begins holding unofficial lunch meetings, issuing directions that contradict the approved roadmap, and telling vendors they speak for the hospital. Some team members comply to stay safe politically. The PMO asks the project manager to keep the project green. Compliance and privacy leads are alarmed about uncontrolled scope promises.

steps.label: Stabilize authority
steps.prompt: What is the project manager's best immediate action?

correct.text: Use governance to appoint an interim sponsor and reaffirm official decision rights
correct.why: Shadow direction is a governance emergency; restore legitimate sponsorship and reaffirm official decision rights.

distractors.text: Escalate the leadership vacuum formally to the PMO and wait for it to name an accountable sponsor before acting
distractors.trap: Procedural deflection: reports the gap and waits while shadow direction keeps flowing.

distractors.text: Send stakeholders a note reaffirming the approved roadmap as the only valid direction
distractors.trap: Too narrow: restates the roadmap without an accountable interim sponsor to enforce it.

distractors.text: Ask the department head to hold their directions until the sponsor returns from leave
distractors.trap: Premature solution: manages one symptom without re-establishing legitimate authority.

steps.explanation: Governance and Stakeholders: restore legitimate sponsorship and reaffirm decision rights when informal power fills a vacuum. Waiting on the PMO, re-sending the roadmap, or asking the influencer to pause each leave the authority gap open.

steps.label: Handle shadow orders
steps.prompt: Vendors already started work from a lunch-meeting promise. What next?

correct.text: Stop unauthorized work, document changes, and route needs through formal change control
correct.why: Unauthorized commitments must enter change control to protect baseline, compliance, and budget.

distractors.text: Fold the vendor's in-progress work into the baseline to avoid disrupting the schedule
distractors.trap: Premature solution: absorbs uncontrolled scope before assessing its value and authority.

distractors.text: Have the vendor email you a scope summary, then continue at your informal nod
distractors.trap: Too narrow: substitutes an informal PM approval for the change-control process.

distractors.text: File a change request but let the vendor keep working until the board formally reviews it
distractors.trap: Procedural deflection: allows live, possibly non-compliant work to continue under the guise of process.

steps.explanation: Scope and Finance governance: bring unauthorized commitments under change control. Folding the work into the baseline, accepting it on an informal nod, or letting it continue while a request waits all leave scope uncontrolled.

steps.label: Repair the team
steps.prompt: Team members who complied with shadow orders fear punishment. How should the project manager respond?

correct.text: Clarify forward expectations, avoid scapegoating, and protect raising governance concerns
correct.why: Psychological safety lets people resist illegitimate direction next time without fear of blame.

distractors.text: Reissue the decision-rights policy and require every team member to acknowledge it in writing
distractors.trap: Too narrow: a written rule without safety will not change behavior under political pressure.

distractors.text: Move on quickly without discussing what happened so the team feels no blame
distractors.trap: Avoidance: skipping the conversation leaves norms unclarified and the fear unaddressed.

distractors.text: Refer the compliance question to HR and tell the team to await guidance
distractors.trap: Procedural deflection: outsources a team-trust repair the project manager should lead.

steps.explanation: People domain: rebuild safety and clear norms so teams follow legitimate governance. A signed policy, quietly moving on, or referring people to HR each fail to restore trust and clarify expectations.

steps.label: Engage the influencer
steps.prompt: The department head still has high interest and useful domain knowledge. Best long-term engagement?

correct.text: Invite them into a formal stakeholder role with clear influence boundaries and channels
correct.why: High-interest stakeholders are best engaged in a defined role with clear boundaries, not ignored or over-empowered.

distractors.text: Give them a standing advisory seat on the steering committee before defining the limits that keep their influence legitimate
distractors.trap: Premature solution: grants influence before the boundaries that keep it legitimate are set.

distractors.text: Route their input only through the product owner to keep them out of decisions
distractors.trap: Too narrow: a single narrow channel sidelines useful domain knowledge without genuine engagement.

distractors.text: Ask the interim sponsor to handle all contact with them so you can avoid friction
distractors.trap: Procedural deflection: offloads the engagement rather than structuring the stakeholder's participation.

steps.explanation: Stakeholder engagement: convert disruptive influence into structured participation with explicit boundaries. An unbounded advisory seat, a single narrow channel, or offloading contact each mishandle a valuable high-interest stakeholder.

title: Ransomware on Cutover Weekend
topic: incident response, risk response, and ethical communication under crisis
scenario: A predictive ERP cutover for a mid-size manufacturer is scheduled over a holiday weekend with a tight freeze window. Saturday morning, the project war room learns that a ransomware incident has hit a connected file server used for migration scripts and some non-production data. Core production plants are not yet encrypted, but IT security declares an incident. The sponsor texts that the market announcement of go-live already went out and failure would be humiliating. Some team members want to keep migrating to save the weekend.

steps.label: Prioritize safety
steps.prompt: What should the project manager do first?

correct.text: Pause risky cutover steps, activate incident response, and follow security's containment lead
correct.why: Containing an active cyber incident with the accountable security team outranks schedule heroics.

distractors.text: Have IT isolate the infected file server, snapshot it for forensics, and let the remaining low-risk migration steps keep running to save the weekend
distractors.trap: Symptom not root cause: contains the visible server but keeps risky work moving before security scopes the blast radius.

distractors.text: Log the ransomware as a new risk and table it for the next change control board
distractors.trap: Procedural deflection: defers a live security incident to paperwork instead of acting now.

distractors.text: Brief the sponsor on schedule impact and ask whether to keep the announcement true
distractors.trap: Too narrow: engages only the schedule stakeholder and skips security, who owns containment.

steps.explanation: Risk response in crisis: contain the active incident with the accountable team before deflecting, patching, or protecting the schedule.

steps.label: Reassess go-live
steps.prompt: Security estimates 48 hours before environments can be trusted. The freeze window ends in 30 hours. Best decision framing?

correct.text: Recommend abort or defer based on residual risk, recovery confidence, and failed-cutover impact
correct.why: Go/no-go is a risk and value decision under uncertainty, not a commitment to a press release.

distractors.text: Escalate the 30-versus-48-hour conflict to the steering committee for their go/no-go ruling
distractors.trap: Procedural deflection: hands up an unframed timing conflict instead of a risk-based recommendation.

distractors.text: Extend the freeze window by a day and notify plant operations of the revised schedule so the cutover can still finish once environments are trusted
distractors.trap: Premature solution: jumps to a scheduling fix before weighing recovery confidence and failure impact.

distractors.text: Ask security to compress recovery into the 30-hour window to hold the plan
distractors.trap: Too narrow: pressures one function to fit the date rather than integrating operational risk.

steps.explanation: Governance of go/no-go: bring an integrated residual-risk recommendation rather than deflecting, patching the schedule, or squeezing one function.

steps.label: Communicate truth
steps.prompt: The sponsor wants external messaging that go-live succeeded while quietly rolling back. What should the project manager do?

correct.text: Refuse false statements and work with sponsor and communications on accurate status
correct.why: Professional ethics require honesty; false go-live claims create legal and trust damage.

distractors.text: Write an accurate holding statement describing the rollback and release it yourself immediately, without waiting for communications or legal review
distractors.trap: Too narrow: honest and prompt, but bypasses the communications and legal functions that own external messaging.

distractors.text: Escalate the wording dispute to the PMO and freeze all messaging until they decide
distractors.trap: Procedural deflection: parks a time-sensitive ethics issue in a committee instead of engaging the sponsor.

distractors.text: Propose delaying further announcements until go-live is genuinely complete and verified
distractors.trap: Premature solution: jumps to deferring messaging before correcting the false impression already created.

steps.explanation: Ethics and communication: correct the record through the functions with standing, not by acting alone, deflecting to a committee, or merely delaying.

steps.label: Recover the plan
steps.prompt: After containment, how should the project re-enter delivery?

correct.text: Update risks, restore a hardened cutover plan with security gates, then re-baseline formally
correct.why: Post-incident recovery must fold new controls into an honestly replanned baseline under change control.

distractors.text: Rebook next weekend's cutover now, then add security controls once the date is locked
distractors.trap: Wrong sequence: commits to a date before replanning controls and re-baselining.

distractors.text: Patch the compromised file server and reuse the original runbook unchanged
distractors.trap: Symptom not root cause: fixes the visible entry point but skips lessons and new gates.

distractors.text: Hand recovery planning to security and resume delivery once they clear the environment
distractors.trap: Too narrow: outsources replanning to one function instead of integrating risk, schedule, and governance.

steps.explanation: Schedule, risk, and governance: fold new controls into an honestly replanned, re-baselined cutover rather than patching one symptom or committing a date first.

title: Discovery Trapped Inside a Fixed-Price Box
topic: contract-delivery mismatch and tailoring commercial approach
scenario: A city awards a fixed-price contract for a resident benefits eligibility redesign. The RFP assumed known requirements, but after award both parties learn policy interpretation varies by office and the true need is discovery-heavy. The vendor staffs for specification delivery; the city product lead wants weekly experiments with residents. Change requests pile up, goodwill erodes, and the vendor threatens to stop non-baseline work. The project manager sits on the city side and must protect public value without pretending uncertainty does not exist.

steps.label: Name the mismatch
steps.prompt: What is the core problem?

correct.text: A fixed-scope commercial model is misaligned with genuinely high-uncertainty discovery work
correct.why: Contract type and delivery approach must fit uncertainty, or conflict and poor value follow.

distractors.text: The change requests are written poorly and need a tighter change-control process
distractors.trap: Symptom not root cause: treats the pile of changes, not the commercial mismatch driving them.

distractors.text: The vendor understaffed discovery and simply needs to add more analysts
distractors.trap: Too narrow: blames staffing and misses that the contract type forbids iterative learning.

distractors.text: The city and vendor simply need clearer requirements workshops to document every policy rule fully up front before building
distractors.trap: Premature solution: assumes requirements are knowable up front, which the discovery-heavy reality contradicts.

steps.explanation: Tailoring: name the structural commercial-delivery mismatch, not the symptoms, staffing, or a requirements-clarity wish.

steps.label: Stabilize near term
steps.prompt: While a commercial fix is negotiated, what delivery approach is best?

correct.text: Time-box joint discovery with clear goals, exit criteria, and a restructure decision point
correct.why: A governed discovery box produces learning without unbounded, unpriced change.

distractors.text: Renegotiate the whole contract to time-and-materials before any discovery is scoped
distractors.trap: Premature solution: jumps to a commercial fix before learning what the discovery actually needs.

distractors.text: Escalate the change-request backlog to the procurement board and pause delivery meanwhile
distractors.trap: Procedural deflection: freezes live work behind a committee instead of structuring interim discovery.

distractors.text: Have the city product lead run weekly resident experiments independently, without vendor involvement or agreed exit criteria
distractors.trap: Wrong sequence: starts learning but excludes the vendor and bounds, recreating uncontrolled change.

steps.explanation: Governance under uncertainty: bound joint discovery now rather than renegotiating blind, freezing work, or running unbounded experiments.

steps.label: Restructure commercially
steps.prompt: What commercial adjustment best fits remaining work?

correct.text: Use a capped discovery phase for uncertain design, then price clearer build increments
correct.why: Hybrid commercial terms share risk in line with how much is actually known at each stage.

distractors.text: Convert the entire remaining contract to firm-fixed-price with a detailed change log
distractors.trap: Too narrow: keeps the failing fixed-price mismatch, just better documented.

distractors.text: Move everything to open-ended time-and-materials to maximize flexibility immediately
distractors.trap: Premature solution: fixes rigidity but drops the cost controls public funds require.

distractors.text: Extend the current contract unchanged and revisit the model at the next annual review
distractors.trap: Procedural deflection: defers the commercial fix while the mismatch keeps burning value.

steps.explanation: Procurement tailoring: share risk in step with knowledge growth instead of preserving the mismatch, dropping all controls, or deferring the fix.

steps.label: Protect residents
steps.prompt: A policy office wants to skip resident testing to hit a political date. Best response?

correct.text: Present eligibility-error and trust risks; recommend a smaller release that still validates users
correct.why: Public-service value requires validating outcomes that affect residents, even under date pressure.

distractors.text: Add QA capacity to keep the full launch on the political date
distractors.trap: Premature solution: throws resources at the date instead of scoping to what can be safely validated.

distractors.text: Log the skipped-testing decision as an accepted risk owned by the policy office
distractors.trap: Procedural deflection: books foreseeable resident-harm as paperwork rather than proposing a safer release.

distractors.text: Run internal staff testing only and defer resident validation to a later post-launch phase to protect the political date
distractors.trap: Too narrow: substitutes staff for the residents whose eligibility is actually affected.

steps.explanation: Business Environment and ethics: scope a validated smaller release rather than buying speed, booking the risk, or testing with the wrong users.

title: When Union Rules Meet the Kanban Board
topic: labor constraints, respectful engagement, and tailored ways of working
scenario: A utility is modernizing field-work scheduling with a hybrid approach: predictive integration to legacy systems and a Kanban flow for the mobile app team. Several key subject-matter experts are unionized field supervisors whose collective agreement limits unpaid after-hours meetings and defines how temporary duties are assigned. Agile coaches schedule frequent workshops outside those bounds. Grievances are threatened. The sponsor still expects continuous discovery with the field. The project manager must keep engagement without violating agreements or ethics.

steps.label: Honor constraints
steps.prompt: What should the project manager do first?

correct.text: Review labor agreements and design lawful participation with labor relations and union input
correct.why: Labor agreements are real constraints; the party with standing must shape a lawful engagement design.

distractors.text: Offer the supervisors overtime pay for evening workshops to resolve the objection
distractors.trap: Premature solution: jumps to a compensation fix before understanding which agreement terms are in play.

distractors.text: Ask HR alone whether the workshops technically breach the collective agreement
distractors.trap: Too narrow: engages only HR and skips the union, the party with standing on the agreement.

distractors.text: Log the grievance threat as a risk and continue workshops until one is filed
distractors.trap: Procedural deflection: defers a live labor issue to the register instead of designing lawful participation now.

steps.explanation: Business Environment and People: let the party with standing shape lawful participation rather than buying, deflecting, or narrowing it away.

steps.label: Redesign participation
steps.prompt: Which participation design best fits the constraints?

correct.text: Schedule discovery in paid duty time, rotate representatives, and use async for non-urgent input
correct.why: Tailoring within labor rules still secures the field knowledge the solution needs.

distractors.text: Survey all field supervisors about their availability before consulting labor relations
distractors.trap: Wrong sequence: gathers input first but skips the representatives who must agree how participation works.

distractors.text: Add the two most senior supervisors to every workshop regardless of their duty schedules to guarantee deep field expertise
distractors.trap: Too narrow: secures expertise but ignores duty-time rules and burns out a narrow group.

distractors.text: Shift all workshops to recorded sessions supervisors watch on their own time
distractors.trap: Premature solution: a tooling fix that still pushes unpaid effort onto workers.

steps.explanation: Stakeholder engagement under constraints: secure field expertise through paid, agreed, transparent participation, not availability surveys, a narrow few, or unpaid recordings.

steps.label: Handle conflict
steps.prompt: A coach insists ceremonies are non-negotiable Scrum and files are more important than contracts. Response?

correct.text: Coach principles over rituals and tailor practices to constraints without abandoning collaboration
correct.why: Agile is values and outcomes, not rigid ceremony compliance that overrides real constraints.

distractors.text: Escalate the dispute to the agile center of excellence for a ceremonies ruling
distractors.trap: Procedural deflection: routes a coachable conflict to a committee instead of addressing it directly.

distractors.text: Redesign every ceremony into asynchronous, documented formats first, then discuss the underlying intent with the coach afterward
distractors.trap: Premature solution: reformats rituals before aligning on the underlying principles.

distractors.text: Let the coach run standard ceremonies but exempt only the unionized supervisors
distractors.trap: Too narrow: patches one group's symptom without resolving the method-versus-constraints principle.

steps.explanation: Tailoring and servant leadership: coach principles over rituals directly rather than escalating, reformatting blindly, or patching one group.

steps.label: Sustain trust
steps.prompt: How should working agreements be made durable?

correct.text: Document participation rules with labor input and review them when cadence or staffing changes
correct.why: Explicit, jointly owned agreements prevent recurring conflict and show respect as conditions change.

distractors.text: Have labor relations sign off on the rules without involving the supervisors themselves
distractors.trap: Too narrow: secures legal cover but misses the workers whose buy-in makes rules stick.

distractors.text: Publish a fixed participation policy now and avoid revisiting it to preserve stability
distractors.trap: Premature solution: locks rules prematurely so they cannot adapt when cadence or staffing shifts.

distractors.text: Track each grievance as it arises and adjust rules case by case afterward
distractors.trap: Symptom not root cause: reacts to grievances individually instead of setting durable agreements.

steps.explanation: People and governance: make jointly owned, transparent agreements durable rather than seeking cover, locking rules prematurely, or reacting case by case.

title: Stage Gates That Strangled the Pilot
topic: governance tailoring for learning-heavy work
scenario: A telecom launches a customer self-care pilot intended to test three value hypotheses in twelve weeks. Corporate PMO applies the enterprise predictive stage-gate standard: full business case, detailed WBS, and document packages designed for multi-year capital programs. The team spends nine weeks on gate artifacts and has three weeks left for building. Early customer interviews already falsified one hypothesis, but the gate packet still describes the original plan. The sponsor asks whether to push through Gate 2 or rethink the approach.

steps.label: Diagnose fit
steps.prompt: What is the project manager's best assessment?

correct.text: Enterprise predictive gates are poorly tailored to a short learning pilot
correct.why: Governance must fit uncertainty and duration; heavy capital gates can strangle pilot learning speed.

distractors.text: The team managed the gate artifacts inefficiently and lost time to rework
distractors.trap: Symptom not root cause: blames execution efficiency instead of the governance-to-work mismatch.

distractors.text: The business case was written too vaguely to survive the Gate 2 review
distractors.trap: Too narrow: focuses on one artifact's quality, not the heavyweight gate model itself.

distractors.text: The pilot simply started building too late after spending time planning
distractors.trap: Wrong framing: treats it as a scheduling slip rather than misfit governance consuming the timeline.

steps.explanation: Governance and tailoring: diagnose the gate-to-work mismatch, not execution efficiency, one artifact, or a scheduling slip.

steps.label: Propose tailoring
steps.prompt: What governance model should be proposed for the pilot?

correct.text: Lightweight learning gates on evidence, spend, risk, and go/pivot/stop decisions
correct.why: Pilot governance should inspect learning, spend, and pivot risk, not documentation completeness.
